title: The lighthouse cook
summary: On a lonely Scottish lighthouse, the keeper and the cook disagree about whether their isolated work still matters.

body.speaker: Fergus
body.text: The supply boat is late again, Innes. Three days late. We have rice, some old potatoes, and two tins of fish. I can make soup, but it will be thin.

body.speaker: Innes
body.text: Thin soup is fine. The light is what matters, not the soup. As long as the lamp turns, the ships are safe.

body.speaker: Fergus
body.text: But the lamp turns by itself now. The new machine does it. Nobody needs us to climb the stairs and light it by hand anymore. Sometimes I think we are only here to eat thin soup.

body.speaker: Innes
body.text: The machine turns the light, yes. But last winter the machine froze. Do you remember? For two nights we turned the lamp by hand, you and me, all night. A ship passed close to the rocks at three in the morning. We saw its lights.

body.speaker: Fergus
body.text: I remember. My arms hurt for a week.

body.speaker: Innes
body.text: That ship had two hundred people on it. If the lamp had been dark, the rocks would have taken them. The machine could not save them that night. We did.

body.speaker: Fergus
body.text: So we are here for the nights the machine fails.

body.speaker: Innes
body.text: Yes. Most nights, we are not needed. We cook, we wait, we watch the grey sea. But the sea does not care about 'most nights'. It only needs us to be wrong about one night, and then there must be two men and a flame.

body.speaker: Fergus
body.text: Then I will make the soup thicker tomorrow. A man who waits for the bad night should not wait on an empty stomach.

annotations.meaningVi: cái máy bị đóng băng / hỏng

annotations.phrase: the rocks would have taken them
annotations.meaningVi: những tảng đá đã có thể nuốt họ (đắm tàu)
chúng ta không được cần đến

grammarNotes.title: Third conditional for an imagined past result
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu điều kiện loại 3 nói về điều KHÔNG xảy ra trong quá khứ và kết quả tưởng tượng của nó. Trong bài: 'If the lamp had been dark, the rocks would have taken them.' (Đèn KHÔNG tối, nên tàu KHÔNG đắm.) Cấu trúc: 'If + had + V3, would have + V3.' Đây là điểm hơi cao hơn A2; người học chỉ cần nhận ra nghĩa: nói về một quá khứ giả định trái với thực tế.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The third conditional talks about something that did NOT happen in the past and its imagined result. From the dialogue: 'If the lamp had been dark, the rocks would have taken them.' (The lamp was NOT dark, so the ship was NOT lost.) Structure: 'If + had + past participle, would have + past participle.' This is slightly above A2; learners only need to recognise the meaning: an imagined past, the opposite of what really happened.

translationVi:
Fergus: Thuyền tiếp tế lại trễ nữa rồi, Innes. Trễ ba ngày. Mình còn gạo, mấy củ khoai cũ, và hai hộp cá. Tôi nấu súp được, nhưng nó sẽ loãng.

Innes: Súp loãng cũng được. Ngọn đèn mới quan trọng, không phải súp. Chừng nào đèn còn quay, tàu thuyền còn an toàn.

Fergus: Nhưng giờ đèn tự quay rồi. Cái máy mới làm việc đó. Chẳng ai cần ta trèo cầu thang và thắp đèn bằng tay nữa. Đôi khi tôi nghĩ ta ở đây chỉ để ăn súp loãng.

Innes: Đúng, cái máy quay đèn. Nhưng mùa đông năm ngoái cái máy bị đóng băng. Anh còn nhớ không? Suốt hai đêm ta quay đèn bằng tay, anh và tôi, cả đêm. Một con tàu đi sát những tảng đá lúc ba giờ sáng. Ta thấy đèn của nó.

Fergus: Tôi nhớ. Cả tuần cánh tay tôi đau nhức.

Innes: Con tàu đó chở hai trăm người. Nếu ngọn đèn tối, những tảng đá đã nuốt họ rồi. Đêm đó cái máy không cứu được họ. Ta đã cứu.

Fergus: Vậy ta ở đây vì những đêm cái máy hỏng.

Innes: Đúng. Hầu hết các đêm, ta không được cần đến. Ta nấu nướng, ta chờ, ta nhìn biển xám. Nhưng biển không quan tâm đến 'hầu hết các đêm'. Nó chỉ cần ta đoán sai về một đêm thôi, và khi đó phải có hai người đàn ông và một ngọn lửa.

Fergus: Vậy ngày mai tôi sẽ nấu súp đặc hơn. Một người chờ cái đêm tồi tệ thì không nên chờ với cái bụng đói.

questions.prompt: What is the problem at the start?
options: The supply boat is three days late and food is low.
questions.explanation: 'The supply boat is late again... Three days late.'

questions.prompt: What does Innes say matters most?
questions.explanation: 'The light is what matters, not the soup.'

options: The new machine turns the lamp by itself now.
questions.explanation: 'The lamp turns by itself now. The new machine does it.'

questions.prompt: What happened to the machine last winter?
What did the two men do when the machine froze?
options: Turned the lamp by hand for two nights.
Called for help on the radio.
questions.explanation: 'For two nights we turned the lamp by hand.'

questions.prompt: What does Fergus remember about that work?
options: His arms hurt for a week

questions.prompt: How many people were on the ship that passed?
questions.explanation: 'That ship had two hundred people on it.'

questions.prompt: According to Innes, why are the men still there?
options: For the nights the machine fails.
questions.explanation: 'So we are here for the nights the machine fails.' — 'Yes.'

questions.prompt: What does Fergus decide to do tomorrow?
options: Wait for the boat at sea.
questions.explanation: 'Then I will make the soup thicker tomorrow.'

questions.prompt: What does Innes mean: 'The sea... only needs us to be wrong about one night'?
options: Even if they are unneeded most nights, one failure could kill people, so they must always be ready.
The sea wants them to leave.
One night a year is enough work.
questions.explanation: Rare disaster justifies constant readiness — that is the point.
questions.hint: Innes's 'most nights' speech, meaning in context.

options: Work that seems pointless most of the time is justified by the rare moment when only a human can act.
questions.explanation: The frozen-machine night is the answer to Fergus's doubt.

cloze.template:
Fergus: The supply boat is late again, Innes. Three days {{b1}}. We have rice, some old potatoes, and two tins of {{b2}}. I can make soup, but it will be thin.
Innes: Thin soup is fine. The {{b3}} is what matters, not the soup. As long as the lamp turns, the ships are safe.
Fergus: But the lamp turns by itself now. The new {{b4}} does it. Nobody needs us to climb the stairs and light it by hand anymore. Sometimes I think we are only here to eat thin soup.
Innes: The machine turns the light, yes. But last winter the machine {{b5}}. Do you remember? For two {{b6}} we turned the lamp by hand, you and me, all night. A ship passed close to the {{b7}} at three in the morning. We saw its lights.
Fergus: I remember. My arms hurt for a week.
Innes: That ship had two hundred {{b8}} on it. If the lamp had been dark, the rocks would have taken them. The machine could not save them that night. We did.
Fergus: So we are here for the nights the machine fails.
Innes: Yes. Most nights, we are not needed. We cook, we wait, we watch the grey {{b9}}. But the sea does not care about 'most nights'. It only needs us to be wrong about one night, and then there must be two men and a flame.
Fergus: Then I will make the soup {{b10}} tomorrow. A man who waits for the bad night should not wait on an empty stomach.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Innes says they exist for the one night the machine fails, even though they are 'not needed' most nights. Is there a role in your community — a guard, a nurse, a volunteer — whose value you only see during an emergency? How should we treat such work when nothing is going wrong?
